title: Scaling Laws for Neural-Network Quantum States
authors: Riccardo Rende, Alessandro Sinibaldi, Luciano Loris Viteritti, Roeland Wiersema, Antoine Georges, Giuseppe Carleo
abstract: Scaling laws, the power-law relations between loss, architecture size, and compute observed in modern neural networks, offer a quantitative way to characterize the complexity of a learning problem, with the exponent governing the decay of the loss reflecting how rapidly additional resources translate into improved accuracy, and thus how hard the target is to learn. Whether an analogous framework can characterize the complexity of physical problems remains open. We address this question for Neural-Network Quantum States, a leading variational approach for strongly correlated quantum many-body systems. Using transformer wave functions to approximate ground states of the $J_1$-$J_2$ Heisenberg model on triangular and square lattices with up to $20\times 20$ sites, we find that the $V$-score, a measure of accuracy of a variational state, decays as a power law in training compute. Under an appropriate rescaling of compute, results for different system sizes collapse onto a single curve, analogous to scaling collapse in critical phenomena. The resulting power law is, to a good approximation, independent of the number of sites, showing that the transformer Ansatz is size-consistent for the systems considered. The exponent decreases systematically with frustration, identifying it as a quantitative measure of representational difficulty of the ground state and establishing scaling laws as a general framework for benchmarking variational ansätze.
summary: This work applies scaling laws from deep learning to variational quantum simulations of spin models. By analyzing how the approximation error decays with training compute, the authors establish a quantitative measure ($\alpha$) that characterizes the intrinsic difficulty of the target quantum ground state. This suggests scaling laws can serve as a general benchmark for variational quantum algorithms.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether the scaling laws observed in modern neural networks can be used to characterize the computational complexity of solving physical problems using Neural-Network Quantum States (NQS).
main_result: The accuracy measure ($V$-score) decays as a power law in training compute, and the scaling exponent systematically decreases with physical frustration, suggesting it quantifies the ground state's representational difficulty.
method: The authors use a variational approach with transformer wave functions to approximate ground states, fitting the resulting error metric ($V$-score) to a power law dependent on training compute and system size.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the $J_1$-$J_2$ Heisenberg model on 2D lattices (square and triangular) to generate target ground states spanning different levels of magnetic frustration.
key_observables: The $V$-score (a measure of variational accuracy), and the scaling exponents ($\alpha,  eta$) derived from the power-law fit of the $V$-score vs. compute.
important_parameters: Frustration level ($J_2/J_1$ ratio), system size ($L 	imes L$ up to $20 	imes 20$), and training compute ($f$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the scaling behavior follows a power law, and that the training compute scales linearly with system size ($f \approx aN$).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the scaling collapse of the $V$-score for various spin Hamiltonians against rescaled compute. Figure 2 illustrates the data collapse procedure for the square $J_1-J_2$ model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of applying ML scaling laws to physics, details the use of transformer wave functions for the $J_1-J_2$ model, presents the power-law fitting and data collapse, and concludes by interpreting the scaling exponent as a measure of physical complexity.
why_interesting: It provides a novel, quantitative framework—the scaling exponent $\alpha$—to benchmark the difficulty of approximating quantum ground states, linking machine learning theory directly to condensed matter physics complexity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Scaling collapse of the V -score for transformer wave function on four two-dimensional spin Hamiltonians: square Heisenberg, triangular Heisenberg, square J1-J2 Heisenberg, and triangular J1-J2 Heisenberg model (see Section II). For each model, V -score data obtained at linear system sizes L = 12, 16, 18, 20 are plotted against the rescaled compute fN −β/α, where f is the training compute in FLOPs and α, β are the exponents of the scaling Ansatz of Eq. (3). After rescaling, data from all system sizes collapse onto a single power law V -score = A f −α N β (dashed black lines), with the fitted exponent α indicated next to each data set. The exponent varies systematically across...

FIG. 2. Illustration of the scaling collapse procedure for the square J1-J2 Heisenberg model. Panel (a): V -score as a function of the training compute f (FLOPs) for linear system sizes L = 12, 16, 20. Panel (b): The same data plotted against the rescaled compute fN −β/α, with α and β taken from Table I, collapse onto a single curve well described by the power law V -score = A f −αN β (dashed black line) [see Eq. (4)].

FIG. 3. V -score as a function of the rescaled compute fN −β/α and corresponding power law fit for different ar- chitectural choices: the transformer architecture with linear patch size b = 4 as in Fig. 1, the architecture with a smaller b = 2, and the architecture with b = 2 and augmented with translation symmetries. The system considered here is the square J1-J2 Heisenberg model with L = 12.

FIG. 4. Cost C of a single forward evaluation of the trans- former wave function on one spin configuration measured in FLOPs as a function of the length of the input sequence n = L2/b2. The transformer architecture considered for this analysis has linear patch size b = 4, number of layers nl = 8 and embedding dimension d = 72. A linear scaling regime of the cost C with n (highlighted in green) and a quadratic scaling regime (highlighted in red) are identifiable.

FIG. 5. V -score as a function of the rescaled compute fN −β/α and corresponding power law fit for optimizations performed using two numbers of Monte Carlo samples, M = 16 384 and M = 20 480. The system considered here is the square J1-J2 Heisenberg with L = 12. The regime where the scaling laws cease to apply is highlighted in grey.
